data:
The Rules, from Teaching Tech Together:
Be kind: all else is details.
Remember that you are not your learners…
…that most people would rather fail than change…
…and that ninety percent of magic consists of knowing one extra thing.
Never teach alone.
Never hesitate to sacrifice truth for clarity.
Make every mistake a lesson.
Remember that no lesson survives first contact with learners…
…that every lesson is too short for the teacher and too long for the learner…
…and that nobody will be more excited about the lesson than you are.


Teach Computing . Org
Pedagogy Quick Reads


From Hello World @raspberrypi
The Big Book of Computing Pedagogy
The Big Book of Computing Content

This is a collection of curriculum design principles that I've invented (or discovered?) since I started teaching programming. Some of them I've used for many years and have been the foundation of my teaching, others are new and I hope to use soon.Inspired by the Pedagogy Quick Reads, I've tried to introduce each of these design principles as something simple and actionable. To learn more about the ideas behind these design princples, you can find them in my notes. The notes are less clear than these pages but will have more detail, links to relevant literature, open questions, and all sorts of linked ideas.

PRISMM is an approach for structuring lessons inspired by PRIMM. PRISMM includes an extra situating step after students have studied a program, but before they write their own similar programs. This step is for exploring how software relates to the wider world. You can visualize the PRISMM process like this:


diagram adapted from primmportal.org
To start using PRISMM in your class you can begin by sharing out any piece of level-appropriate code as a study reference, with some support learners can get used to guide their own study with the PRISMM steps. Doing this regularly with a series of programs that progress alongside your learners is a (relatively) low-effort way to practice teaching with PRISMM. If you would like more guidance to get started, try using existing resources for PRIMM and adding a little extra time for a situating discussion. PRIMM Portal has a collection of lesson plans for Python.The PRISMM steps in more detail:
data.predict#Predict: Students discuss a program and predict what it might do, drawing or writing out what they think will be the output. At this level, the focus is on the function of the code.
data.run#Run: Students run the program so that they can test their prediction and discuss in class.
data.investigate#Investigate: The teacher provides a range of activities to explore the structure of the code; this involves activities such as tracing, explaining, annotating, and debugging. Study Lenses is has many features to support investigating code.
data.situate#Situate: Discuss who wrote the program, why they wrote it, who would use the program, and why they would use it. Seeing the intentions behind and effects of software can help learners understand their digital world, and gain a sense of agency.
data.modify#Modify: Students edit the program to change its functionality or purpose via a sequence of increasingly challenging exercises; the transfer of ownership moves from the code being ‘not mine’ to ‘partly mine’ as students gain confidence by extending the function of the code
data.make#Make: Students design a new program that uses the same structures as the code they studied, but is designed to solve a differnet problem or is adapted for a different end user.

data: What you're reading is a big work in-progress; I'm trying to organize what I've learned, experienced and designed for teaching programming into something you will find helpful.The chapters on this page have short, practical introductions to some ideas & tools I've found helpful. Everything except the Awesome Teaching are original work. If you find anything especially interesting and want to learn more about its inspiration or how it connects to other ideas, search for the same key words in my notes.
data.enough-about-me#Enough About Me: I love teaching programming and how to do it better. I'm curious how we can better translate evidence-based teaching/learning strategies into tools, materials and guides that are actually useful for teachers and learners. Over the last 7 years I've been doing just that; running and designing programming courses from the ground up inspired by my students & fellow teachers, computing education research, and by the tight constraints placed on small schools and non-profits.I am also curious how we can develop content authoring/generation tools, class management tools, and study tools that make it realistic for learners & small organizations to manage their own curriculum without sacrificing pedagogical value. I call this an Ownable Code School when everything necessary to run a programming course (with or without teachers) can be set up by an alumni with only the skills learned in the course (more about this in my notes). So far we have a couple functioning prototypes but could use some help making them more reliable.If any of this sounds fun to you, send me an email. We can probably find something to work on together.
data.thank-yous#Thank Yous:
Thank you to all of my students & alumni whose patience and collaboration made this possible, all the developers who shared their experience and teaching advice, and all my colleagues who made sure I had the time to focus on education. I'd also like to thank the computing education researchers who were gracious enough to answer my emails or have a call with me.A big and special thank you to Yoshi Malaise for helping make my ideas presentable, and for generally being an inspiration to work with. Another special thank you to Adam Leskis for big ideas and fun calls. And one more for Cody Kommers who dreamed way too big with me at step 0 of my teaching.
I'll limit this to teaching-related thank you's for now, or there would be no more room!
